content.problemStatement:
You have an N×MN \times MN×M grid. The grid can be filled with the values 000 and 111 only. Initially, **all** the cells of the grid are filled with value 000.

You start from the cell (1,1)(1,1)(1,1). When you are at position (i,j)(i,j)(i,j), you can do one of the following:


Change A(i,j)A_{(i,j)}A(i,j)​ to 111 and go to cell (i+1,j+1)(i+1,j+1)(i+1,j+1).
Keep A(i,j)A_{(i,j)}A(i,j)​ as 000 and go to cell (i+1,j)(i+1,j)(i+1,j) or (i,j+1)(i,j+1)(i,j+1).
If, at some point, you are out of the grid, the process ends.

Let SSS denote the set of all **distinct** matrices that can be created using the method explained above.

Let f(T)f(T)f(T) be the number of ways to place **any** (possibly zero) number of rooks in the grid TTT such that both the following conditions are satisfied:


No two rooks attack each other.
No rook is placed in a cell (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) such that T(i,j)=1T_{(i,j)} = 1T(i,j)​=1.
Find the value of ∑f(T)\sum f(T)∑f(T) where T∈ST \in ST∈S. Since the answer can be huge, print it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.

**Note:**


Two matrices AAA and BBB are different if there exists some cell (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) such that A(i,j)≠B(i,j)A_{(i,j)} \neq B_{(i,j)}A(i,j)​=B(i,j)​.
Two ways of arranging rooks is different if there exists a cell (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) such that a rook is placed in the cell (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) in one way and the cell (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) is empty in the other way.
Two rooks attack each other if they share the same row or the same column.

Input Format
The first and only line of input consists of two integers NNN and MMM, the dimensions of the grid.



Output Format
Print a single line containing one integer - the value of ∑f(T)\sum f(T)∑f(T) where T∈ST \in ST∈S modulo 998244353998244353998244353.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
We can create 333 distinct matrices:

[1,0][1,0][1,0] : Start from (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) and change A(1,1)A_{(1,1)}A(1,1)​ to 111. Thus, you move to cell (2,2)(2,2)(2,2). This cell is out of the matrix. Therefore, the process ends.
[0,1][0,1][0,1]: Start from (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) and keep A(1,1)A_{(1,1)}A(1,1)​ as 000. Move to cell (1,2)(1,2)(1,2). Change A(1,2)A_{(1,2)}A(1,2)​ to 111. Thus, you move to cell (2,3)(2,3)(2,3). This cell is out of the matrix. Therefore, the process ends.
[0,0][0,0][0,0]: This matrix is generated in three cases:

Start from (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) and keep A(1,1)A_{(1,1)}A(1,1)​ as 000. Move to cell (1,2)(1,2)(1,2) and keep A(1,2)A_{(1,2)}A(1,2)​ as 000. Move to cell (1,3)(1,3)(1,3). This cell is out of the matrix. Therefore, the process ends.
Start from (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) and keep A(1,1)A_{(1,1)}A(1,1)​ as 000. Move to cell (1,2)(1,2)(1,2) and keep A(1,2)A_{(1,2)}A(1,2)​ as 000. Move to cell (2,2)(2,2)(2,2). This cell is out of the matrix. Therefore, the process ends.
Start from (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) and keep A(1,1)A_{(1,1)}A(1,1)​ as 000. Move to cell (2,1)(2,1)(2,1). This cell is out of the matrix. Therefore, the process ends.



Thus, S={[1,0],[0,1],[0,0]}S = \{[1,0], [0,1], [0,0]\}S={[1,0],[0,1],[0,0]}. Calculating the number of ways to place rooks while satisfying both conditions:

T=[1,0]T = [1,0]T=[1,0]: There are 222 ways to place rooks:

Place 000 rooks in the matrix.
Place 111 rook in the cell (1,2)(1,2)(1,2). Since there is only one rook, no two rooks attack each other. Also, the cell in which the rook is placed has value 000.


T=[0,1]T = [0,1]T=[0,1]: There are 222 ways to place rooks:

Place 000 rooks in the matrix.
Place 111 rook in the cell (1,1)(1,1)(1,1). Since there is only one rook, no two rooks attack each other. Also, the cell in which the rook is placed has value 000.


T=[0,0]T = [0,0]T=[0,0]: There are 333 ways to place rooks:

Place 000 rooks in the matrix.
Place 111 rook in the cell (1,2)(1,2)(1,2). Since there is only one rook, no two rooks attack each other. Also, the cell in which the rook is placed has value 000.
Place 111 rook in the cell (1,1)(1,1)(1,1). Since there is only one rook, no two rooks attack each other. Also, the cell in which the rook is placed has value 000.



The total answer is the sum of number of ways in which we can place rooks over all the matrices. Thus, the answer is 2+2+3=72+2+3=72+2+3=7.

Explanation:
This example has a beautiful explanation. Unfortunately, it is too long to write.
